title: Chapter 3: The First Supper
content: The archive was kept at eighteen degrees Celsius and forty-five percent humidity — the temperature of a morgue, Jane thought, and just as respectful of its dead.

She'd waited three days for this. Three days of polite orientation tours and introductions to staff whose names she'd forget, three days of HR paperwork signing with a pen that leaked, three days of Edward Rochester's conspicuous absence while her curiosity curdled into impatience. But now she stood at the threshold of the room that mattered, and her pulse quickened the way it always did when she was about to touch something real.

The archive occupied the château's east wing, a vaulted stone chamber that some long-ago Rochester had either the foresight or the madness to climate-control decades before such things were standard. Floor-to-ceiling glass cases lined every wall, their interiors lit in soft amber that made the garments inside look like sleeping creatures. Garment bags hung on rolling racks — hundreds of them — each tagged with handwritten notations in faded ink. Bolts of fabric stood in wooden cradles along the far wall, organized by decade, their selvages yellowed and crisp. The air smelled of lavender and mothballs and something older — the mineral tang of stone that had held its breath for centuries.
Jane pulled on her cotton gloves. They were fresh from the box she'd packed in London, white and tight across her knuckles. She flexed her fingers. Ready.

She began with the earliest pieces. Thornfield had been founded fifteen years ago — a lifetime in fashion, an eyeblink in craft — and the archive held a nearly complete record from the first collection forward. Jane lifted the first garment bag from its rack and laid it on the examination table with the care of a surgeon prepping a patient. Inside: a tailored jacket from the debut collection, charcoal wool with a single oxidized brass button. She turned it inside out.

Her fingers knew what to do before her mind caught up. They traced the seam allowance — generous, hand-felled, with tiny irregularities that marked the work of a human hand rather than a machine. She counted stitches per inch. Fourteen. Consistent but not mechanical; there was a micro-variation in tension that a computer would never produce. The thread was silk, the hand-stitching impeccable. She turned the cuff and found — there — a tiny detail that made her pause. A line of contrast stitching on the interior facing, invisible from the outside. Decorative, yes, but subversively so. A secret flourish meant only for the maker and the wearer. A hidden laugh.

She worked methodically through the morning, pulling pieces from the racks in chronological order, photographing construction details with her phone's camera, making notes in the small leather journal she kept for exactly this purpose. The silence of the archive was its own kind of music — the soft hush of cotton gloves on wool, the whisper of tissue paper, the occasional creak of the old table as she leaned her weight against it. She lost herself in the work the way she always did, her skepticism humming like a tuning fork beneath the surface of her concentration, resonating against every seam and stitch and silent claim.
By midday, she had examined nineteen pieces spanning the first five years of Thornfield's output. By two o'clock, she'd moved into the recent work. And by three, she saw it.

Jane laid out two blazers side by side on the table — one from the third collection, one from last season. Both were extraordinary. Both were immediately recognizable as Thornfield: the same clean silhouette, the same architectural relationship between structure and drape, the same instinct for a shoulder line that could make a woman look like she could conquer a boardroom or a battlefield with equal ease. The design language was continuous, a single creative voice speaking across a decade and a half.
But the hands were different.

She picked up the older piece and ran her gloved fingers along the interior seams. There it was again — that impulsiveness. The seam allowance varied slightly from one section to the next, as if the maker had changed her mind mid-construction and adjusted on the fly. There was playful asymmetry in the pocket placement, so subtle that a customer would never notice, but Jane noticed. And inside the left cuff, a tiny embroidered motif — a thorn, no bigger than her thumbnail, stitched in thread the exact color of the shell fabric. A ghost signature. A maker's mark that said *I was here*.

She turned to the newer piece. The construction was flawless — more than flawless, actually. It was precise in a way that bordered on the devotional. Every stitch was uniform, every seam allowance mathematically consistent. The asymmetry was gone, replaced by a more controlled, more architectural sense of balance. But the underlying genius was the same: the way the shoulder was engineered to create that particular Thornfield silhouette, the way the fabric was cut to exploit its grain. The mind was the same. The hands were not.

Jane photographed the seam transitions, taking close-ups of the older hand-stitching next to the newer work. She wrote in her journal: *Consistent design language. Shifted execution ~5 yrs ago. Same mind, different hands? Different hands, same mind? The craft didn't evolve — it was replaced.*
She stared at the two blazers on the table. One whispered. The other spoke in measured tones. Both said Thornfield. But the first one said it like a secret, and the second one said it like a fact.
Edward found her at dinner.

He appeared in the doorway of the small dining room looking apologetic and expensive, his dark hair slightly damp from a shower, wearing a cashmere sweater the color of old whiskey. "I'm told I've been a terrible host," he said, crossing the room to where she sat at one end of a table that could have seated twelve. "Business in Paris. Unavoidable. I'm sorry."
"Don't be," Jane said. "I've been perfectly happy in the archive."

"I heard." He settled into the chair diagonal to hers — not at the far end of the table, not beside her, but at that intimate diagonal that said *I want to look at you while we talk*. A servant she hadn't seen before placed a plate before each of them and melted back into the shadows. Lamb, rosemary, something deeply caramelized. The candles on the table threw shifting shadows across the walls, and the smoke was sweet and fatty, tallow and beeswax mingling in the warm air.

Edward asked about her day with the focused attention of a man who had trained himself to seem fascinated. He leaned forward when she spoke, nodded at the right moments, laughed at her deconstruction of fashion's hypocrisy with a warmth that felt — almost — genuine. She told him about the archive, about the construction techniques, about the difference between a French seam and a Hong Kong finish, and he listened as though she were reciting poetry rather than technical specifications. It was flattering. It was practiced. Jane enjoyed the flattery while noting the practice.

The wine arrived — a Burgundy, old enough to have shed its fruit for something earthier. Jane tasted oak and tannin and the faintest memory of dark soil after rain. Edward swirled his glass, watched the legs form on the crystal, and said: "I understand you've been studying our archive. Finding anything interesting?"

The question was casual. The way he set down his glass was not — it was too careful, too deliberate, a gesture designed to look absent-minded while actually being the opposite. And his eyes, in the candlelight, were not casual at all. They watched her the way a chess player watches the board mid-game: reading the position, calculating the next three moves.
Jane reached for her wine. She needed the pause. She needed to decide whether to show him what she'd found or keep it hidden, and she needed to decide in the space of a sip of Burgundy.
She chose the middle path — the technical observation, diplomatically framed. She could always reveal more later. She could never un-reveal what she'd already said.
"There's a remarkable consistency in your design language," she said, "but the execution shifted around five years ago. New atelier team?"
His hand paused on his wine glass.
One beat.
Jane counted it. One beat — not the startled freeze of genuine surprise, not the fumble of someone caught off-guard. A single, measured beat, like a rest in a musical score. A deliberate silence.

Then he smiled — warmly, easily, a smile that rearranged his face into something almost boyish. "We evolved. Thornfield grew up, and so did the craft." He paused, swirling his wine. "Innovation requires... unconventional methods sometimes."

The ellipsis was audible. A fraction of a second where something flickered behind his eyes, there and gone before she could name it. Then he redirected with the fluid grace of a man who had spent his life controlling conversations: "Speaking of unconventional — do you know the story of how we sourced our Mongolian cashmere?"

He told it well. A journey to the Gobi, a nomadic herder who could identify cashmere quality by touch alone, a handshake deal sealed with fermented mare's milk under a sky so wide it made you dizzy. Edward Rochester told stories the way he did everything — with total command of the room, with charm that felt like gravity, with the absolute certainty that you would listen and you would be moved. And Jane listened, and she was moved, and she noted the redirect with clinical precision.

He had not answered her question. He had answered a different question — one about sourcing and adventure and the romance of craft — and he had made it feel like the answer to hers. It was a magician's technique: draw the eye, misdirect, make the coin disappear while the audience watches the other hand.

Jane noted the redirect. She noted the pause. She noted that it was exactly one beat — calculated, not spontaneous. A natural pause would have been messier, longer or shorter, accompanied by some involuntary tell: a blink, a swallow, a shift in posture. Edward's pause was one clean beat, a professional silence, the kind of thing you rehearsed.
She smiled, asked a follow-up question about the herder, and let him talk.

After dinner, he walked her from the dining room through the long stone corridor that led back toward the east wing. Their footsteps echoed in tandem, hers quicker and lighter, his measured and deliberate. The corridor was cold after the warmth of the dining room, the stone walls exuding the kind of chill that seeped through cashmere and into bone.
He stopped at the archive door. Not her suite door — the archive door.

He turned to face her. In the dim light of the corridor sconces, his features were all angles and shadows, and she thought: *this is what he looks like when he's not performing. This is the face behind the charm.*
"Our history is in those garments, Jane," he said. "I trust you'll treat it with respect."

The word *respect* landed with a slight emphasis — not enough to constitute a threat, just enough to reclassify the sentence from a statement into a directive. It was the linguistic equivalent of a hand on the shoulder: light, almost affectionate, but with the implicit promise of pressure if needed.

Jane looked at him. She could feel the journal in her pocket, the weight of it, the importance of its contents. She could feel the photographs on her phone, still no Wi-Fi to back them up, existing only as data in a device she held in her hand.
"I always treat garments with respect," she said. "It's the people who make them I have questions about."

His smile didn't waver. It was a good smile — warm, open, the kind that invited you to trust it. But something behind his eyes recalculated. She saw it happen: a micro-shift, like a lock clicking into a new position. He was still smiling. He was still performing. But the performance had adjusted to account for new information, and the new information was her.
"Goodnight, Jane," he said.
"Goodnight, Edward."
He turned and walked away down the corridor. His footsteps receded until they were swallowed by the stone.

Alone in her suite, Jane sat on the edge of the bed and typed into her phone's notes app. Still no Wi-Fi — the château's signal reached the common areas but died at the bedroom doors, as if the walls themselves were keeping secrets.
She typed:
*Same mind, different hands. Or same hands, different name. The craft didn't evolve — it was transferred. The question isn't how. It's who.*

She stared at the screen until the backlight dimmed, then went out. In the darkness, she lay back on the bed and stared at the ceiling instead — plaster medallions, crown molding, the faint outlines of a fresco that had faded beyond recognition. Someone had painted something here once, and someone had let it disappear. That seemed like the kind of detail that should mean something.

Below the floorboards, the scissors were silent tonight. No silver ringing, no phantom snipping. Whatever lived in the basement of Thornfield — whatever made the sounds that Grace Poole's locked door guarded — it was quiet. And the quiet was somehow worse than the noise, because the noise told you something was there. The quiet could mean anything.
Jane closed her eyes. Behind them, she saw the two blazers on the archive table — one whispering, one measured. One with a thorn hidden inside the cuff. One with nothing hidden at all.

She'd found the first thread. She could feel it, thin and strong, running through the fabric of Thornfield's history like a selvage edge — the structural seam that holds the cloth together while the weaver works. Pull it and the whole thing might unravel. Or it might hold.
The question was whether she could pull it without letting Edward see her hands.

She switched off the lamp. The château settled around her with the small sounds of old buildings — the tick of contracting stone, the whisper of air through gaps in masonry, the distant, indefinite creak of somewhere below. Jane lay in the dark and thought about hands. About who had made the early work — the impulsive, playful, secretly signed work — and who was making it now. About the one-beat pause and what it cost a man who never miscalculated.
About the thorn inside the cuff, and what it meant that the newer pieces carried nothing hidden at all.

Somewhere in this house, someone was not being credited. Someone's hands were making garments that carried another name. Jane was sure of it the way she was sure of a French seam — you couldn't see it from the outside, but you could feel it if you knew where to press.
She just needed to find the right seam and push.
The ceiling offered no answers. The scissors stayed silent. And Jane Eyre lay awake in the dark of Thornfield, holding her thread, waiting for morning.
